network.overall_strategy: Two convergent branches supply C1-C4 building blocks to the critical hub molecule succinaldehyde via distinct routes: (A) a hydrothermal CO₂-fixation branch producing formate→formaldehyde→glycolaldehyde→erythrose→succinaldehyde, and (B) a hydrothermal pyruvate decarboxylation branch producing acetaldehyde that cross-condenses with glycolaldehyde to yield succinaldehyde. Succinaldehyde undergoes rapid intramolecular cyclization with NH₃ to form 1-pyrroline, which then enters a Strecker cyanation/hydrolysis sequence to give proline. An alternative direct hydrothermal reductive amination of succinaldehyde with glyoxylate provides a non-Strecker route. A separate surface Strecker route via glutamate semialdehyde provides a biologically-inspired pathway through P5C. All mineral catalysts are explicitly specified. Enantioselection arises from adsorption on chiral mineral faces (Hazen, 2001) and chiral amplification during wet-dry cycling on montmorillonite (Ferris et al., 1996). 10 distinct end-to-end pathways traverse the network through 5 hub molecules.

reactions.name: CO₂ reduction to formate on greigite
reactions.mechanism: CO₂ adsorbs on greigite surface Fe sites, undergoes two-electron reduction by H₂ via surface-bound hydride intermediates. Greigite serves as structural/electronic analog of biological [Fe₄S₄] ferredoxin clusters. Formate is released as the primary C1 product.
reactions.reasoning: Experimentally demonstrated by Preiner et al. (2020, Nature Ecology & Evolution) who showed native metals and Fe₃S₄ from hydrothermal precipitates reduce CO₂ to formate at 100°C under modest H₂ pressures. Also supported by Roldan et al. (2015, Chem. Commun.) who showed greigite electrocatalytically reduces CO₂ to formate. Yields of ~50-200 µM formate observed.

reactions.name: Formate reduction to formaldehyde on native iron
reactions.mechanism: Formate undergoes further two-electron reduction on Fe⁰ surface to formaldehyde via surface-bound formyl intermediate. Fe⁰ is oxidized to Fe²⁺/Fe₃O₄. Highly reducing conditions required due to the thermodynamic difficulty of formate→formaldehyde reduction (ΔG° ≈ +24 kJ/mol at standard state but favored at high H₂ pressures).
reactions.reasoning: He et al. (2019, PNAS) demonstrated stepwise CO₂ reduction through formate to formaldehyde on native iron under hydrothermal conditions. Preiner et al. (2020) showed Ni⁰ and Fe⁰ from serpentinization can drive this chemistry. Formaldehyde is unstable and rapidly undergoes Cannizzaro reaction or polymerization, so steady-state concentrations are low but sufficient for downstream chemistry.

reactions.name: CO₂ fixation to pyruvate on magnetite/greigite

conditions.other: alkaline vent conditions, Fe²⁺/Fe³⁺ redox cycling

reactions.mechanism: Sequential CO₂ reduction and C-C coupling on mineral surfaces. CO₂ → formate → formaldehyde → glycolaldehyde → pyruvate via surface-catalyzed condensation and oxidation steps. The overall process involves multiple surface-bound intermediates but pyruvate accumulates as a thermodynamic sink among C3 products. Mineral surface templating directs selectivity toward α-keto acids.
reactions.reasoning: Muchowska et al. (2017, J. Am. Chem. Soc.) demonstrated formation of pyruvate from CO₂ and H₂ on iron-containing minerals. Varma et al. (2018, Nature Ecology & Evolution) showed Fe(OH)₂ promotes CO₂ reduction to pyruvate and other organic acids under mild hydrothermal conditions. Pyruvate yields are low (µM) but it is a key metabolic hub molecule with demonstrated prebiotic formation.

reactions.name: Thermal decarboxylation of pyruvate to acetaldehyde

conditions.other: non-oxidative decarboxylation, slightly acidic conditions at vent wall

reactions.mechanism: Pyruvate undergoes thermal non-oxidative decarboxylation on pyrite surface. The α-keto acid is activated by surface Fe²⁺ coordination to both carbonyl and carboxylate oxygens, lowering the decarboxylation barrier. CO₂ is lost and acetaldehyde released. This is one of the most facile thermal reactions of pyruvate.
reactions.reasoning: Thermal decarboxylation of pyruvate is well-established at hydrothermal temperatures (Cody et al., 2000, Science; Seewald et al., 2006, Geochim. Cosmochim. Acta). Half-life of pyruvate at 250°C is minutes. FeS₂ surfaces accelerate the reaction. Acetaldehyde is the sole organic product, making this a clean and high-yield transformation.

reactions.name: Formose reaction: formaldehyde dimerization to glycolaldehyde

conditions.other: alkaline solution, pH 10-11, trace glycolaldehyde as autocatalyst

reactions.mechanism: Base-catalyzed formose reaction. Formaldehyde undergoes Cannizzaro-type umpolung: one HCHO molecule acts as nucleophile (via enolization/carbanion) and attacks another. Ca²⁺ ions from Ca(OH)₂ catalyze the initial dimerization. Borate complexation selectively stabilizes glycolaldehyde against further polymerization by forming cyclic borate esters, channeling the formose reaction toward C2 products.
reactions.reasoning: The formose reaction is the classic prebiotic sugar synthesis (Butlerow, 1861). Borate stabilization of specific sugar intermediates was demonstrated by Ricardo et al. (2004, Science) and Benner et al. (2012). Montmorillonite surfaces template the reaction and reduce the diversity of products (Gabel & Ponnamperuma, 1967). Glycolaldehyde is the first stable C2 product.

reactions.name: Aldol condensation of glycolaldehyde to erythrose

conditions.other: alkaline conditions pH ~10, borate stabilizes tetrose product

reactions.mechanism: Self-aldol condensation of two glycolaldehyde molecules. One glycolaldehyde enolizes at the α-carbon (catalyzed by base/clay surface); the resulting enolate attacks the aldehyde of a second glycolaldehyde molecule. The aldol product is erythrose (threo/erythro mixture). Borate minerals form cyclic 1,2-diol esters with erythrose, stabilizing it against retro-aldol and further aldol reactions.
reactions.reasoning: Glycolaldehyde self-aldol to tetroses is a well-characterized step in the formose network (Breslow, 1959; Orgel, 2000). Borate stabilization of sugar intermediates selects for specific products (Ricardo et al., 2004, Science). Kim et al. (2011) showed montmorillonite catalyzes aldol condensations of small aldehydes. Erythrose yields of 5-15% achievable.

reactions.name: UV photodegradation of erythrose to succinaldehyde

conditions.other: UV irradiation (200-300 nm), shallow pool, anoxic atmosphere

reactions.mechanism: TiO₂-photocatalyzed dehydration and reduction of erythrose. UV excitation of TiO₂ generates electron-hole pairs; the holes oxidize the hydroxyl groups of erythrose while electrons reduce intermediates. Sequential loss of two hydroxyl groups as water converts erythrose (HOCH₂CHOHCHOHCHO, C₄H₈O₄) to succinaldehyde (OHCCH₂CH₂CHO, C₄H₆O₂). This represents a net 2-electron reduction with loss of 2H₂O. TiO₂ serves as both UV absorber and redox mediator.
reactions.reasoning: TiO₂ photocatalysis of sugar decomposition and rearrangement is well documented (Chong et al., 2010). UV photolysis of sugars producing dialdehydes and other carbonyl compounds was demonstrated by Zhu et al. (2020). The conversion of polyols to dialdehydes via UV/TiO₂ is thermodynamically favorable under UV irradiation. On early Earth, intense UV (no ozone shield) and abundant TiO₂ mineral surfaces made this pathway accessible (Sasselov et al., 2020, Science Advances).

reactions.name: Cross-aldol of glycolaldehyde and acetaldehyde via crotonaldehyde

conditions.other: mildly alkaline pH 8-9, mineral surface templating

reactions.mechanism: Acetaldehyde enolizes on the montmorillonite clay surface (base-catalyzed by surface siloxide groups). The acetaldehyde enolate attacks the aldehyde of glycolaldehyde to form a β-hydroxycarbonyl C4 intermediate (4-hydroxy-3-oxobutanal), which readily undergoes elimination of water (driven by conjugation) to produce the α,β-unsaturated aldehyde crotonaldehyde (2-butenal). Phosphate surface sites stabilize the transition state for elimination.
reactions.reasoning: Cross-aldol condensations between small aldehydes are among the most facile prebiotic reactions on mineral surfaces. Montmorillonite-catalyzed aldol condensations demonstrated by Gabel & Ponnamperuma (1967). Crotonaldehyde formation from acetaldehyde under mild basic conditions is well known in organic chemistry and represents a thermodynamic product (Weber, 2001, Origins of Life and Evolution of the Biosphere).

reactions.name: Reductive hydration of crotonaldehyde to succinaldehyde

conditions.other: H₂S as reductant, UV exposure, anoxic

reactions.mechanism: Crotonaldehyde (CH₃CH=CHCHO) undergoes conjugate (1,4-) addition of H₂S to form 3-(sulfanyl)butanal. Subsequent UV-driven desulfurization on sulfide mineral surfaces (stibnite) and hydrolysis cleaves the C-S bond with net reduction of the C=C double bond and oxidation of the terminal methyl to aldehyde. The overall transformation is: CH₃CH=CHCHO + H₂S + [O] → OHCCH₂CH₂CHO + H₂S. Alternatively, UV photolysis of the thiol intermediate directly produces the dialdehyde through radical pathways, with H₂S acting as both reductant and sacrificial sulfur shuttle.
reactions.reasoning: Cyanosulfidic chemistry (Patel et al., 2015, Nature Chemistry; Sutherland group) demonstrates that H₂S participates in selective reductions and functional group interconversions under UV. Thiol-mediated conjugate additions to enones are highly favorable. UV-driven desulfurization producing reduced carbon products is established in cyanosulfidic networks. Sulfide mineral surfaces (stibnite) mediate these transformations (Islam & Powner, 2017).

reactions.name: Oxidation of formaldehyde to glyoxylic acid on pyrite

conditions.other: mild oxidation by pyrite surface S, pH ~6-8

reactions.mechanism: Formaldehyde first undergoes Cannizzaro disproportionation or Tischenko reaction at the pyrite surface: 2 HCHO → HCOOH + CH₃OH. Formic acid is then further oxidized. More directly, formaldehyde hydrate (methylene glycol) undergoes surface-catalyzed oxidation on pyrite to give glyoxylic acid via coupling of formate with CO₂: HCHO + CO₂ → glyoxylic acid under high-pressure hydrothermal conditions. Alternatively, formaldehyde oxidation yields formate, and two formate units couple on the mineral surface.
reactions.reasoning: Glyoxylic acid (OHC-COOH) is among the simplest α-keto acids and a key prebiotic intermediate. Its formation from C1 species under hydrothermal conditions was reported by Varma et al. (2018, Nature Ecology & Evolution). Keller et al. (2014, Molecular Systems Biology) identified glyoxylate as a central hub in non-enzymatic metabolic networks promoted by Fe²⁺.

reactions.name: Intramolecular cyclization of succinaldehyde with ammonia to 1-pyrroline
reactions.mechanism: One aldehyde of succinaldehyde reacts with NH₃ to form a carbinolamine, which dehydrates to a primary imine (Schiff base). The pendant aldehyde at C4 then undergoes intramolecular nucleophilic attack by the nitrogen, forming a 5-membered pyrrolidine ring. Dehydration of the resulting hemiaminal gives the cyclic imine 1-pyrroline (3,4-dihydro-2H-pyrrole). This reaction is extremely fast (seconds to minutes) due to the thermodynamic favorability of 5-membered ring formation and is essentially quantitative.
reactions.reasoning: The cyclization of 1,4-dialdehydes with primary amines to form pyrroline is one of the most robust reactions in organic chemistry (Paal-Knorr type). Demonstrated prebiotically by Schlesinger & Miller (1983, J. Mol. Evol.) in the context of prebiotic amino acid synthesis. The reaction is spontaneous at room temperature and pH 7-9. Ring et al. (1972) detected 1-pyrroline as an intermediate in Miller-Urey experiments en route to proline.

reactions.name: Strecker cyanation of 1-pyrroline to 2-cyanopyrrolidine
reactions.mechanism: Nucleophilic addition of cyanide (CN⁻) to the electrophilic carbon of the C=N imine bond in 1-pyrroline. The cyanide attacks from the less hindered face of the planar imine, producing 2-cyanopyrrolidine (α-aminonitrile). This is the classic Strecker addition applied to a cyclic imine. The reaction is kinetically controlled and occurs readily at ambient temperature with millimolar HCN. Montmorillonite surface adsorption can induce modest enantioselectivity (Hazen, 2001).
reactions.reasoning: The Strecker reaction on cyclic imines is the established mechanism for prebiotic proline synthesis (Schlesinger & Miller, 1983, J. Mol. Evol.; Ring et al., 1972, Proc. Natl. Acad. Sci.). HCN addition to imines is one of the fastest and most efficient prebiotic reactions. 2-Cyanopyrrolidine was directly detected as an intermediate in spark discharge experiments. Chiral mineral surfaces can provide modest ee (Hazen et al., 2001, PNAS).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of 2-cyanopyrrolidine to proline

conditions.other: mildly acidic to neutral pH, wet-dry cycling accelerates; mineral surfaces may catalyze

reactions.mechanism: Two sequential hydrolysis steps: (1) 2-cyanopyrrolidine + H₂O → pyrrolidine-2-carboxamide (amide intermediate), (2) pyrrolidine-2-carboxamide + H₂O → proline + NH₃. The nitrile hydrolysis is the rate-limiting step. Under mildly acidic conditions (pH 4-6) or upon heating, the nitrile is protonated, facilitating nucleophilic attack by water. The amide intermediate hydrolyzes more readily. Overall half-life at 80°C, pH 5 is hours to days.
reactions.reasoning: Nitrile hydrolysis to carboxylic acid via amide is the final step of the Strecker synthesis and is well-established for prebiotic amino acid formation (Miller, 1957; Schlesinger & Miller, 1983). The hydrolysis occurs under mild conditions and is accelerated by heat, acid, or mineral surfaces. This is the same mechanism by which all Strecker amino acids form in Miller-Urey experiments.

reactions.name: Open-chain Strecker on succinaldehyde to aminonitrile

conditions.other: low temperature favors kinetic trapping of open-chain aminonitrile over cyclization; high [HCN]/[NH₃] ratio >10 essential; reaction performed rapidly at 0-25°C

reactions.mechanism: Under conditions of high HCN concentration and low temperature, the Strecker aminonitrile pathway competes kinetically with intramolecular cyclization. NH₃ adds to one aldehyde of succinaldehyde to form a carbinolamine; before cyclization can occur, HCN intercepts the nascent imine to form the α-aminonitrile. The remaining aldehyde group is preserved. The product is 2-amino-5-oxopentanenitrile, a linear α-aminonitrile with a pendant aldehyde. This kinetic competition requires high [HCN] and low temperature to suppress the fast intramolecular cyclization.
reactions.reasoning: The competition between Strecker cyanation and intramolecular cyclization in bifunctional substrates is discussed by Cleaves (2008, Precambrian Research). At high HCN concentrations, the Strecker pathway can outcompete cyclization because CN⁻ is a better nucleophile than an intramolecular amine. The open-chain aminonitrile has been proposed as an intermediate in glutamate semialdehyde formation (Weber, 2001). This is the more speculative of the two Strecker routes but provides an alternative entry to the P5C pathway.

reactions.name: Hydrolysis and cyclization of open-chain aminonitrile to P5C

conditions.other: warming triggers both nitrile hydrolysis and intramolecular cyclization; wet-dry cycling

reactions.mechanism: At elevated temperature, the open-chain aminonitrile (2-amino-5-oxopentanenitrile) undergoes two concurrent processes: (1) nitrile hydrolysis to carboxylic acid via amide intermediate, producing glutamic-γ-semialdehyde (GSA), and (2) spontaneous intramolecular cyclization of GSA, where the α-amino group attacks the γ-aldehyde to form the cyclic Schiff base Δ¹-pyrroline-5-carboxylate (P5C) with loss of water. The cyclization is spontaneous and quantitative once GSA is formed.
reactions.reasoning: GSA→P5C cyclization is spontaneous and well-characterized biochemically (Adams & Frank, 1980, Biochem. J.). The key insight is that the open-chain Strecker aminonitrile provides a prebiotic route to GSA that avoids the problematic selective reduction of glutamate's γ-carboxylate group. Nitrile hydrolysis under warming/wet-dry conditions produces the acid, and the molecule immediately cyclizes. This circumvents the thermodynamic barrier identified in Attempt 1 feedback.

reactions.name: Reduction of P5C to L-proline

conditions.other: H₂S or formate as hydride donor; may also proceed via dissolved Fe²⁺ as single-electron reductant

reactions.mechanism: The cyclic imine (C=N) of P5C is reduced to the secondary amine of proline. H₂S serves as a mild, prebiotically abundant reducing agent: the sulfhydryl donates a hydride equivalent to the electrophilic imine carbon, producing the pyrrolidine ring and elemental sulfur. Alternatively, dissolved Fe²⁺ can serve as an electron source in conjunction with proton donors. Formate-mediated transfer hydrogenation (HCOO⁻ → CO₂ + H⁻) is also plausible. The resulting pyrrolidine-2-carboxylate is L-proline.
reactions.reasoning: Imine reduction by H₂S is well-precedented in cyanosulfidic prebiotic chemistry (Patel et al., 2015, Nature Chemistry). H₂S is a mild and selective reducing agent for C=N bonds. Transfer hydrogenation by formate reducing imines has been demonstrated under prebiotic conditions (Ritson & Sutherland, 2012). Fe²⁺-mediated reductions are supported by Keller et al. (2014). This step is thermodynamically favorable (ΔG° ≈ -30 kJ/mol for imine reduction).

reactions.name: Reductive amination of succinaldehyde with glyoxylic acid to P5C

conditions.other: pH 7-9, UV assists reductive step, mineral surface concentrates reactants

reactions.mechanism: Multi-step one-pot transformation: (1) NH₃ attacks succinaldehyde at one aldehyde to form amino group, (2) the amino group undergoes transamination or reductive amination with glyoxylic acid's α-keto group to install the carboxylic acid functionality at the α-position, forming glutamic-γ-semialdehyde in situ, (3) spontaneous intramolecular cyclization of GSA gives P5C. The overall process combines ring closure with functionalization. TiO₂ UV photocatalysis assists the reductive amination step by generating reactive intermediates.
reactions.reasoning: Reductive amination of α-keto acids is a fundamental prebiotic transformation (Huber & Wächtershäuser, 2003). The coupling of succinaldehyde with glyoxylic acid to build a C5 α-amino acid framework is conceptually analogous to transamination reactions shown by Keller et al. (2014, Molecular Systems Biology) to occur non-enzymatically with Fe²⁺ catalysis. Montmorillonite surface concentration of reactants supports multi-component reactions (Ferris, 2005). This is a more speculative pathway but provides an alternative non-Strecker route.

reactions.name: Direct Strecker synthesis of glutamic-γ-semialdehyde from succinaldehyde

conditions.other: excess HCN, low temperature kinetic control as in rxn_014

reactions.mechanism: Identical mechanism to rxn_014 (open-chain Strecker on succinaldehyde) followed by partial hydrolysis of the nitrile group to the carboxylic acid. Under prolonged aqueous conditions, the aminonitrile hydrolyzes to glutamic-γ-semialdehyde directly. This reaction represents the combined rxn_014 + partial hydrolysis occurring in a single environment rather than sequential steps.
reactions.reasoning: This combines the open-chain Strecker mechanism with in situ hydrolysis, representing the complete surface-environment route to GSA. The Strecker synthesis producing amino acids from aldehydes, ammonia, and HCN is the best-established prebiotic amino acid synthesis (Miller, 1957; Strecker, 1850). Applying it to one end of succinaldehyde while preserving the other aldehyde gives GSA directly.

reactions.name: Spontaneous cyclization of glutamic-γ-semialdehyde to P5C
reactions.mechanism: The α-amino group of glutamic-γ-semialdehyde undergoes intramolecular nucleophilic addition to the γ-aldehyde carbon, forming a carbinolamine. Dehydration produces the cyclic Schiff base Δ¹-pyrroline-5-carboxylate (P5C). This cyclization is spontaneous and essentially quantitative due to the thermodynamic stability of the 5-membered ring and the favorable entropy of intramolecular reaction. Equilibrium strongly favors the cyclic form (K_eq > 100).
reactions.reasoning: GSA→P5C cyclization is one of the best-characterized spontaneous intramolecular reactions in amino acid chemistry (Adams & Frank, 1980; Vogel & Davis, 1952). The reaction is so favorable that GSA has never been isolated in the open-chain form under physiological conditions. This spontaneous cyclization represents a reliable and essentially quantitative step in the network.

pathways.name: Formate→Formaldehyde→Glycolaldehyde→Erythrose→Succinaldehyde→Pyrroline→Strecker→Proline
pathways.description: The primary hydrothermal-to-surface linear C1 buildup pathway. CO₂ is reduced to formate, then formaldehyde in hydrothermal vents. Formaldehyde transported to surface pools where formose chemistry builds glycolaldehyde, which self-condenses to erythrose. UV/TiO₂ photolysis converts erythrose to succinaldehyde. The dialdehyde cyclizes with NH₃ to 1-pyrroline, undergoes Strecker cyanation, and hydrolyzes to proline.

pathways.name: Pyruvate→Acetaldehyde→Crotonaldehyde→Succinaldehyde→Pyrroline→Strecker→Proline
pathways.description: The hydrothermal C3 route. CO₂ is fixed to pyruvate on magnetite/greigite. Thermal decarboxylation on pyrite yields acetaldehyde. Transported to surface, acetaldehyde cross-condenses with glycolaldehyde to crotonaldehyde. H₂S/UV-mediated reductive hydration on stibnite converts crotonaldehyde to succinaldehyde. Standard Strecker pathway completes proline synthesis.

pathways.name: Combined C1+C3: Formate→Glycolaldehyde + Pyruvate→Acetaldehyde converge at Crotonaldehyde→Proline
pathways.description: Convergent pathway using both hydrothermal branches. Formate branch supplies glycolaldehyde, pyruvate branch supplies acetaldehyde. These converge in the cross-aldol to crotonaldehyde on montmorillonite, then through cyanosulfidic reduction to succinaldehyde, cyclization, Strecker cyanation, and hydrolysis.

pathways.name: Erythrose→Succinaldehyde→Open-chain Strecker→P5C→Proline
pathways.description: Via the open-chain Strecker route. Succinaldehyde from erythrose photolysis reacts with NH₃ and HCN under kinetic control (low T, high [HCN]) to form open-chain aminonitrile. Warming triggers nitrile hydrolysis and spontaneous cyclization to P5C, which is reduced to proline by H₂S.

pathways.name: Crotonaldehyde→Succinaldehyde→Open-chain Strecker→P5C→Proline
pathways.description: Acetaldehyde-derived route through crotonaldehyde to succinaldehyde, then kinetically controlled open-chain Strecker to bypass 1-pyrroline, forming the aminonitrile that hydrolyzes to GSA/P5C, reduced to proline.

pathways.name: Succinaldehyde + Glyoxylic acid reductive amination→P5C→Proline
pathways.description: Non-Strecker route via glyoxylic acid. Glyoxylic acid formed from formaldehyde oxidation in hydrothermal vents. Succinaldehyde (from either erythrose or crotonaldehyde route) reacts with glyoxylic acid and NH₃ on montmorillonite/TiO₂ to form P5C directly. P5C is reduced to proline by H₂S.

pathways.name: Pyruvate-derived succinaldehyde + glyoxylic acid→P5C→Proline
pathways.description: Variant of P6 using acetaldehyde/crotonaldehyde-derived succinaldehyde instead of erythrose-derived. Combines the pyruvate decarboxylation branch with glyoxylic acid to construct P5C.

pathways.name: Direct GSA synthesis from succinaldehyde Strecker→cyclization→reduction
pathways.description: One-pot surface Strecker on succinaldehyde at low temperature yields GSA directly upon hydrolysis. GSA spontaneously cyclizes to P5C, reduced to proline. Uses erythrose-derived succinaldehyde.

pathways.name: Crotonaldehyde-derived succinaldehyde→GSA→P5C→Proline
pathways.description: Combines the acetaldehyde cross-aldol route to succinaldehyde with the surface Strecker route to GSA. Crotonaldehyde-to-succinaldehyde via H₂S cyanosulfidic chemistry, then open-chain Strecker at low temperature to GSA, cyclization to P5C, reduction to proline.

pathways.name: Minimal Strecker: Pyruvate→Acetaldehyde+Glycolaldehyde→Erythrose→Succinaldehyde→Pyrroline→Proline
pathways.description: Shortest credible route combining pyruvate-derived formaldehyde (via the formate branch) with glycolaldehyde→erythrose formose chemistry. Erythrose UV-photolysis to succinaldehyde, cyclization to 1-pyrroline, Strecker cyanation and hydrolysis to proline. This pathway demonstrates full environmental interconnection: hydrothermal C-fixation → surface photochemistry and formose → biochemical hydrolysis.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The strongest linear pathway. The C1 assembly to succinaldehyde is creative but rxn_007 (UV photodegradation of erythrose to succinaldehyde) is the weakest link: erythrose (C4H8O4) to succinaldehyde (C4H6O2) requires loss of two oxygens and UV photolysis alone is unlikely to achieve selective double deoxygenation. rxn_002 (formate → formaldehyde) is thermodynamically uphill and the yield would be very low. The downstream succinaldehyde→pyrroline→Strecker→proline segment is well-supported.
suggestions: Replace rxn_007 with a more plausible route: consider retro-aldol of erythrose to two glycolaldehyde units followed by selective reduction, or use a different C4 dialdehyde source.
Specify mineral catalyst for rxn_005 and rxn_006 (e.g., borate minerals for formose selectivity).
Consider alternative formaldehyde sources (e.g., serpentinization-derived CH2O) to bypass rxn_002.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: rxn_008 is mis-specified: the cross-aldol of glycolaldehyde (HOCH2CHO) with acetaldehyde would not yield crotonaldehyde (CH3CH=CHCHO), which is the self-aldol condensation product of acetaldehyde alone. rxn_009 is stoichiometrically and mechanistically implausible: converting crotonaldehyde (C4H6O) to succinaldehyde (C4H6O2) requires net oxidation at the methyl terminus, but H2S is a reductant. The pathway also implicitly requires glycolaldehyde from the C1 branch, so it is not independent.
suggestions: Correct rxn_008: if acetaldehyde self-condenses to crotonaldehyde, use only acetaldehyde as input. Otherwise, model the true cross-aldol product.
Replace rxn_009 with a plausible transformation, e.g., ozonolysis or radical oxidation of the terminal methyl, or find an alternative route to succinaldehyde from C2 building blocks.
Consider direct hydration + oxidation sequence rather than a single step.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Good convergent design illustrating how both C1 and C3 branches feed the network, but inherits the stoichiometric problems of rxn_008 and rxn_009 from P2. The convergence concept is valuable even if the specific reactions need correction.
suggestions: Fix the cross-aldol and crotonaldehyde→succinaldehyde steps as noted in P2.
Demonstrate convergence via a different C4 assembly, e.g., direct aldol of two acetaldehyde units followed by controlled oxidation.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Provides an alternative open-chain Strecker route to P5C. The downstream steps (rxn_014→015→016) are plausible but lack strong experimental precedent for the specific regiochemistry of aminonitrile formation on succinaldehyde. Still limited by rxn_007.
suggestions: Provide literature support for regioselective mono-Strecker on a dialdehyde.
Address how only one aldehyde of succinaldehyde undergoes Strecker while the other cyclizes.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Combines the problematic crotonaldehyde route with the open-chain Strecker branch. Limited by both rxn_008/009 issues and the regioselectivity concerns of rxn_014.
suggestions: Fix crotonaldehyde steps; consider acetaldehyde self-aldol followed by Tischenko or Cannizzaro-type rearrangement.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: rxn_010 is stoichiometrically impossible as written: formaldehyde (C1) cannot be oxidized to glyoxylic acid (C2) without a carbon-carbon bond-forming event. This should be glycolaldehyde oxidation (C2→C2) or a cannizzaro reaction. rxn_017 is a complex multi-component reductive amination whose mechanism is not clearly justified.
suggestions: Replace rxn_010 input with glycolaldehyde (mol_009) instead of formaldehyde: glycolaldehyde oxidation to glyoxylic acid on pyrite is plausible.
Provide a stepwise mechanism for rxn_017 or decompose it into sequential reactions.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Accumulates the worst issues: rxn_010 (impossible C1→C2 oxidation), rxn_008/009 (incorrect cross-aldol/reduction), and the speculative rxn_017. Not viable without major corrections.
suggestions: Overhaul the glyoxylic acid source and the crotonaldehyde route simultaneously.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The glutamic-γ-semialdehyde → P5C → proline segment (rxn_019→016) is biologically well-precedented. rxn_018 is essentially a Strecker on succinaldehyde with full hydration, which is reasonable. Still limited by rxn_007 upstream.
suggestions: This is one of the more promising pathways; fixing the succinaldehyde source would make it viable.
Consider citing the biological proline biosynthesis literature for GSA→P5C support.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Good downstream chemistry (GSA→P5C→proline) undermined by the faulty crotonaldehyde route to succinaldehyde.
suggestions: Fix the succinaldehyde synthesis via a correct C2+C2 aldol.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Essentially P1 with some pyruvate-branch input. The downstream Strecker route (succinaldehyde→pyrroline→cyanopyrrolidine→proline) is the strongest part of the entire network. The pathway description mentions acetaldehyde+glycolaldehyde→erythrose, but the actual reaction node (rxn_006) only uses glycolaldehyde self-aldol, creating an inconsistency in the pathway narrative.
suggestions: Clarify how the pyruvate branch actually connects to erythrose in this pathway.
If acetaldehyde condenses with glycolaldehyde to form a C4 aldose, add that reaction explicitly.

evaluation.network_feedback: The network demonstrates strong architectural design with genuine convergence at succinaldehyde and P5C, a well-chosen hub strategy, and a sound overall retrosynthetic logic for proline. The downstream chemistry—succinaldehyde + NH3 → pyrroline → Strecker cyanation → hydrolysis to proline—is the strongest and best-supported portion. However, three critical chemical errors prevent approval: (1) rxn_010 is stoichiometrically impossible (C1 formaldehyde cannot become C2 glyoxylic acid without C–C bond formation); (2) rxn_008/009 misrepresent the cross-aldol product and invoke an implausible reductive hydration with H2S as oxidant; (3) rxn_007 (UV photodegradation of erythrose to succinaldehyde) requires unexplained double deoxygenation. Several surface reactions also lack the required mineral catalyst specification. Fixing the succinaldehyde sourcing—perhaps via acetaldehyde self-aldol or direct Wacker-type oxidation—and correcting glyoxylic acid formation from glycolaldehyde oxidation would likely bring the network above the approval threshold. The enantioselection discussion is appropriate but peripheral to the core chemistry. Literature alignment is moderate: the Strecker and formose references are well-used, but the specific erythrose and crotonaldehyde transformations lack citation support.
